answer:
**Johann Gottlieb Fichte**, in *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens* (A Demand for Freedom of Thought from the Princes of Europe), **Chapter 2**, satirizes princely truth-by-fiat in this way:

> Therefore, imitate your worthy models entirely, you princes; reject today what you commanded to be believed yesterday, and authorize today what you rejected yesterday, so that they never become unaccustomed to the idea that your will alone is the source of truth. You have, for example, for too long willed that one equals three; they have believed you, and unfortunately, they have become so accustomed to it that they have long since withheld the gratitude due to you, and believe they discovered it themselves. Avenge your authority; command, just once, that one equals one—not, of course, because the opposite contradicts itself, but because you will it.[^1]

This occurs in the context of Fichte mocking rulers' claims to define truth arbitrarily, contrasting it with the wisdom of early Christian emperors who frequently altered doctrine with each reign to keep subjects reliant on authority alone.[^2]

[^1]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [paragraph](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/#p-ed12aa).
[^2]: Johann Gottlieb Fichte, *Zurückforderung der Denkfreiheit von den Fürsten Europens*, [Chapter 2](/works/johann-gottlieb-fichte-zuruckforderung-der-denkfreiheit-bookde/02/translation/).
